svg.title: How Inky Bird Frame works
svg.desc: Runtime architecture drawn as a field-journal plate. Internet services sit at the top: observation services (iNaturalist, eBird, BirdWeather), licensed reference and research sources, and OpenAI services for Codex generation and review. The controller initiates HTTPS (TCP 443) to those services. On the trusted private network, the Raspberry Pi display node initiates HTTP GET requests to the controller's configured port (8793 in the example configuration) and the controller returns approved catalog metadata and PNG plate images. The display node verifies checksums, applies the rotation mode, and drives the Inky Impression panel over GPIO/SPI. A closing note warns that the controller port is unauthenticated and must stay off the public internet; optional SSH (TCP 22) reaches the display from a setup computer.
svg.text: Codex CLI runs on the controller

g.text: Discovers, creates, reviews, and serves approved plates
Runs on a macOS or Linux computer, Raspberry Pi 4/5, or an AMD64/ARM64 Docker host

svg.text: Wi-Fi, Ethernet, routed private network, or VPN
Display opens HTTP GETs to the
Controller needs a stable name or address; the display can use ordinary DHCP

g.text: Downloads, verifies, selects, and shows approved plates
Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W with header recommended; any supported 40-pin Raspberry Pi works

svg.text: The service has no built-in authentication or TLS, so never expose its configured port to the
internet. Across untrusted networks, use a VPN or an authenticated TLS reverse proxy.
Optional display administration: SSH (TCP 22) from your setup computer
